name: FBI Fabricated MLK's Sex Allegations
summary: The claim that the FBI's sexual allegations against Martin Luther King Jr. — the affairs, the group-sex claims, and the disputed 1964 Willard Hotel note that King "looked on, laughed and offered advice" during an assault — were fabricated by the Bureau to destroy him, rather than a distorted record of real surveilled conduct. The FBI's campaign to discredit King (RFK-authorized wiretaps, the anonymous 1964 "suicide letter") is documented fact; whether it invented the sexual material specifically is unverified — the key claim rests on a handwritten agent annotation while the underlying tapes stay court-sealed until 2027.

evidence_for.text: The most explosive claim — the Willard Hotel "looked on, laughed and offered advice" line — rests on a seven-word handwritten marginal annotation on an FBI memo, not on the audio or even the typed summary.
FBI agents could only hear the bugged rooms, so 'laughed and offered advice' is a subjective transcription by a hostile Bureau, per Yale historian Beverly Gage.

William Sullivan, who oversaw the material, stated a willingness to go beyond 'legalistic proofs that would stand up in court'; King's lawyer Clarence Jones calls the annotation 'almost indecipherable' with no eyewitnesses.
COINTELPRO has a documented record of forging and planting false material against other targets, establishing the Bureau's capability and willingness.
MLK confidant Andrew Young: 'I never saw anything, and I have never found anybody who said they saw something personally.'

evidence_against.text: Historian David Garrow, after combing ~54,000 pages of released FBI files, read the summaries as crediting the allegations as real conduct rather than Bureau invention.

No forged tape, contradicted informant, or internal FBI admission of inventing the sexual material has ever been identified — the fabrication claim rests on inference from motive, not direct evidence.
The underlying audiotapes remain sealed at the National Archives until 2027, so neither the allegations nor the fabrication claim can currently be independently verified.

name: Modern Hawaiian Culture Is a Tourist Reconstruction
aliases: Hawaiian culture was reinvented for tourism
summary: The claim that the Hawaiian culture visitors meet today is largely a sanitized reconstruction built by (mostly non-Native) outsiders for tourism rather than authentic surviving Indigenous heritage — that the language, hula, and 'astronavigator' wayfinding were softened and repackaged, and that showpieces like botanical gardens are white-developed. The historical substrate is real (a ~90% post-contact population collapse, the near-death of the Hawaiian language under an 1896–1987 school ban, and genuine tourism commodification), but the load-bearing attribution is inverted: the 1970s–80s Hawaiian Renaissance was Native Hawaiian–led, wayfinding is an authentic Pacific tradition, and the 'haka' the claim invokes is Māori, not Hawaiian.

evidence_for.text: Introduced disease cut the Native Hawaiian population by roughly 90% in the century after 1778 contact.
Hawaiian was banned as a school-instruction language from 1896 to 1987; native speakers fell to ~2,000, and today only ~1–2% of residents have meaningful fluency.
Native scholars and activists document how the tourism industry commodifies and softens Hawaiian culture for visitors.
The Hawaii Tropical Botanical Garden was carved from overgrown jungle by a mainland businessman, Dan Lutkenhouse, starting in 1977.

evidence_against.text: The 1970s Hawaiian Renaissance — the Merrie Monarch hula revival, Pūnana Leo language immersion, the Kahoʻolawe protests — was led by Native Hawaiians reclaiming suppressed traditions, not reconstructed by white outsiders.
Polynesian non-instrument wayfinding is an authentic, sophisticated Indigenous tradition; its revival came from master navigator Mau Piailug teaching Nainoa Thompson, not tourist invention.
The haka is Māori (New Zealand), not Hawaiian — the Hawaiian forms are hula and haʻa — so it was never part of Hawaiian culture to be 'removed'.
Generalizing from one privately-developed garden ignores Native-stewarded institutions like the Bishop Museum and ongoing Indigenous loko iʻa (fishpond) restoration.
